When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. var_functions.query_db:10.title: Best beginner book.  Been looking for something like this for a long time.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Looked online for years for something like this.  It's the best I've seen.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Greet book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Lots of great information. Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Book of Love series
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Overpriced for the size
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Book is small and feels cheap.  I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

args.code:
# Load children's books data
children_books_data = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
with open('var_functions.query_db:22', 'r') as f:
    import json
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print('Loaded data:')
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books_data)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Get children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books_data]
print(f'Children book IDs: {len(children_book_ids)}')

# Map reviews to books
book_ratings = {}
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this review belongs to a children's book
    # Need to compare purchase_id with book_id
    if purchase_id in children_book_ids:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f'Found {len(book_ratings)} children\'s books with reviews')

# Calculate average ratings for each book
import statistics
book_averages = {}
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews to be meaningful
        avg_rating = statistics.mean(ratings)
        book_averages[book_id] = {
            'average_rating': avg_rating,
            'review_count': len(ratings)
        }

# Filter books with average rating >= 4.5
qualified_books = []
for book_id, stats in book_averages.items():
    if stats['average_rating'] >= 4.5:
        # Find book title
        book_title = None
        for book in children_books_data:
            if book['book_id'] == book_id:
                book_title = book['title']
                break
        if book_title:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_title,
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(stats['average_rating'], 2),
                'review_count': stats['review_count']
            })

qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: x['average_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nQualified books:')
for book in qualified_books:
    print(f"  {book['title']} (ID: {book['book_id']})")
    print(f"    Rating: {book['average_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Best beginner book.  Been looking for something like this for a long time.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Greet book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Lots of great information. Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Book of Love series
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Overpriced for the size
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Book is small and feels cheap.  I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

var_functions.query_db:10.text: Lots of great information. Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

args.code:
import json

# Load children books data (this is not a file path, but direct result)
children_books = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter 
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results
print('\nQualified Children\'s Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"{book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

# Prepare result for return 
__result__ = []
if qualified:
    __result__ = qualified
else:
    __result__ = 'No books found meeting criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(__result__))

val_args.code:
import json

# Load children books data (this is not a file path, but direct result)
children_books = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter 
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results
print('\nQualified Children\'s Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"{book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

# Prepare result for return 
__result__ = []
if qualified:
    __result__ = qualified
else:
    __result__ = 'No books found meeting criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(__result__))

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

# Map book IDs to titles

for book in children_books:
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Calculate ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Filter books with avg rating >= 4.5 and at least 2 reviews
qualified = []

            qualified.append({

            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Book of Love series

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

args.code:
import json

children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Load reviews data from file path stored in storage
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Map book IDs to titles for children's books
book_id_to_title = {}
for book in children_books:
    book_id_to_title[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Group ratings by book ID
book_ratings = {}
for review in reviews_2020:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    # Check if this purchase_id matches a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_id_to_title:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:  # Need at least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_id_to_title[book_id],
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

# Sort by rating descending
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: x['average_rating'], reverse=True)

# Output result
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified_books))

"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

val_args.code:
import json

children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Load reviews data from file path stored in storage
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Map book IDs to titles for children's books
book_id_to_title = {}
for book in children_books:
    book_id_to_title[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Group ratings by book ID
book_ratings = {}
for review in reviews_2020:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    # Check if this purchase_id matches a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_id_to_title:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:  # Need at least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_id_to_title[book_id],
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

# Sort by rating descending
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: x['average_rating'], reverse=True)

# Output result
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified_books))

args.code:
import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))

var_functions.query_db:10.text: Lots of great information. Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

val_args.code:
import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))

args.code:
import json

# Get the stored results
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's books
children_book_ids = set()
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    children_book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Filter reviews for children's books only
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group ratings by book
book_ratings = {}
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    if book_id not in book_ratings:
        book_ratings[book_id] = []
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    review_count = len(ratings)
    if review_count >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / review_count
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': review_count
            })

# Sort by average rating descending, then by review count
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

result_data = qualified_books if qualified_books else 'No books found meeting the criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Greet book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Lots of great information. Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Book of Love series
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Overpriced for the size
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Book is small and feels cheap.  I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

args.code:
import json

# Load reviews to examine purchase_id patterns
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Get all purchase_ids
purchase_ids = set(r['purchase_id'] for r in reviews)
print(f'Total unique purchase_ids: {len(purchase_ids)}')

# Show sample of purchase_ids
print('Sample purchase_ids:', list(purchase_ids)[:10])

# Check if any start with 'bookid'
bookid_purchases = [pid for pid in purchase_ids if str(pid).startswith('bookid')]
print(f'Purchase_ids starting with bookid: {len(bookid_purchases)}')
print('bookid purchases:', bookid_purchases[:20])

# Get children's books IDs
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
print(f'Children book IDs: {children_book_ids[:10]}')

# Find intersection
intersection = set(children_book_ids) & set(purchase_ids)
print(f'Intersection (books with reviews): {len(intersection)}')
print('Intersection IDs:', intersection)

# Check reviews for children books
children_reviews = [r for r in reviews if r['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids]
print(f'Reviews for children books: {len(children_reviews)}')

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_reviews = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_reviews[review['purchase_id']].append(float(review['rating']))

print('\nBook review counts:')
for book_id, ratings in sorted(book_reviews.items(), key=lambda x: len(x[1]), reverse=True):
    print(f'  {book_id}: {len(ratings)} reviews')

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
"Dead Silence" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Book of Love series
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Overpriced for the size
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Book is small and feels cheap.  I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
There are so many things to love about "Dead Silence," by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.
var_functions.query_db:10.text: There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great “who-done-it”.  It certainly held my attention.  Great detective work!

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Gift Recipient LOVES this book
var_functions.query_db:10.text: I purchased this cookbook for my SIL Francofile who is crazy about this book. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. WINNER.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Rare Pics
var_functions.query_db:10.text: Very good collection of photographs, but little else. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles.  Pricier than I would have liked, but that's on me. Supply and demand.

var_functions.query_db:10.title: Great how to book for the beginner and advanced photographers
var_functions.query_db:10.text:
David Busch's guide is very easy to use. The instructions are in layman terms and his explanations are very understandable. He recognizes that not everyone is familiar with the terminology of photography. His definitions and "how to" instructions makes it easy follow. His "tips" are a great help as well.
I highly recommend this guide for anyone interested in digital photography

Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about "Dead Silence" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.

args.code:
import json

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Create sets for comparison
children_ids = set([b['book_id'] for b in children_books])
purchase_ids = set([r['purchase_id'] for r in reviews_2020])

# Find matching books (books that have reviews)
matching_books = children_ids.intersection(purchase_ids)
print('Matching book count:', len(matching_books))

# Count reviews per book
book_review_counts = {}
for review in reviews_2020:
    pid = review['purchase_id']
    if pid in children_ids:
        book_review_counts[pid] = book_review_counts.get(pid, 0) + 1

# Show books with most reviews
sorted_counts = sorted(book_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
print('Top books by review count:')
for book_id, count in sorted_counts[:10]:
    print(' ', book_id, ':', count)

# Calculate average ratings
book_ratings = {}
id_to_title = {b['book_id']: b['title'] for b in children_books}

for review in reviews_2020:
    pid = review['purchase_id']
    if pid in children_ids:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if pid not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[pid] = []
        book_ratings[pid].append(rating)

# Find qualified books
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:
        avg_val = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg_val >= 4.5:
            qualified.append({
                'title': id_to_title[book_id],
                'avg_rating': round(avg_val, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: -x['avg_rating'])

print('Qualified books count:', len(qualified))
for book in qualified:
    print(' ', book['title'])
    print('   Rating:', book['avg_rating'], 'Reviews:', book['review_count'])

result = qualified if qualified else []
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))
